other_observers: My father, Hugh Gillilan, was farther away from the bird and did not see if for as long, primarily noticing the warbler/vireo size and the bright yellow undersides of the bird.; So far as I am aware, there were no other observers of this bird beyond my father and me.
specific_location: Woods behind Lamar Community College ("LCC"), approximately midway through on north/south axis, where the woods were starting to transition to the shrubbier south portion; the bird was observed towards the western edge (closest to the college buildings).
description: Somewhat stubby (shorter/rounder) appearance as compared to a warbling vireo, but roughly the same size. Thick, rounded, slightly hooked bill (easily noted in comparison to the sharp bill of a probable female Nashville warbler seen in association with the vireo). Medium-gray cap, well defined white supercilium extending back and forward of the eye, with dark eyeline (approximately same hue as the cap) extending to and including the lores. Undersides mostly a very bright yellow with some mixed white, with the throat and undertail coverts being particularly bright yellow, the belly showing a more mixed yellow/white, but without any sharp distinction between those areas. Mantle and wing coverts a grayish olive, wings slightly darker, and without visible wing bars. Somewhat short-looking tail, possibly due to more prominent/longer undertail coverts.Behaviors: The bird was foraging in close association with a warbler, 5-10 feet above the ground in a shrubby tree, having dropped down from a somewhat taller adjacent broadleaf tree. Active, but not as much so as the warbler, and with more pauses. I did not observe any tail wagging/dipping or other body-specific movements. Movement pattern while foraging was what I considered to be "classic vireo," with no particularly unique movements that I observed.Call: Not singing or calling.Plumage: Adult breeding
